entry.title: Re: S/4HANA upgrade relevant report version check
entry.content:
Hello,


Yes, these activities may be possible by applying SAP Notes only, but it depends on your current system component versions.


Digitally Signed SAP Notes
Requires specific SNOTE / SAP_BASIS / Kernel prerequisites. If your system is too old, only SAP Notes may not be enough.


ATC Check
Can usually be enabled by SAP Notes, but required SAP_BASIS and ST-PI/ST-A/PI versions must be available.


SAP Readiness Check
Requires implementing SAP Notes and running data collectors. Some minimum support package levels may be required.


SI-Check / Simplification Item Check
Usually delivered via SAP Notes, but also depends on system release and support package level.


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: How to add a check for requisitioner for purchase req...
entry.content:
Hello @Aavil 


Use enhancement/BAdI at PM order save before database update, and raise an error message to stop save/PR creation and return to IW31/IW32.
Recommended enhancements to check


BAdI WORKORDER_UPDATE

Method: AT_SAVE
Use it to stop order save with error message.
But if operation/component data is missing, read current order buffer using standard PM order APIs/function modules.

 

User exit IWO10009

Enhancement: IWO10009
Purpose: Check PM order before save
This is commonly used for validation during IW31/IW32 save.

 

BAdI ME_PROCESS_REQ_CUST

If PR is created from PM order, validate requisitioner at PR item level.
Method: PROCESS_ITEM / CHECK
Raise error to stop PR creation.


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: JSON Payload Download Implementation in RAP Application
entry.content:
Hello @Yash_Rudraksha 


In RAP, don’t download JSON directly from ABAP action.
Instead, return the JSON string from RAP action and trigger download in the Fiori/UI5 side.
RAP action output
Create action with result:
action DownloadJson result [1] $self;


RAP action prepares JSON,
UI5 downloads JSON file,
ABAP RAP action cannot directly open browser download dialog
Same payload can be reused for API call and download


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: SCU3
entry.content:
Hello @Gajey 


Yes, you can create a Z ALV report, but don’t read DBTABLOG-LOGDATA directly. It is stored as LRAW/encoded table record data, so direct SELECT will not give old/new values properly. Use SAP standard logic/FM instead. DBTABLOG stores table change logs and LOGDATA is the modified data record in LRAW format.


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: How I can control MOQ in a sales order not by SKU but...
entry.content:
Your requirement is not clear and need more details.  Answer the below questions
 
1. Scope of MOQ Control
Should MOQ apply to the entire order total (all SKUs combined) or to each SKU line item?  Do you want MOQ rules to apply universally or only for specific customers, regions, or order types?
 
2. Business Logic
If an order contains multiple SKUs, should MOQ be enforced on the combined total quantity or individually per SKU?  What should happen if MOQ is not met?
  • Block the order submission
  • Show a warning message
  • Auto-adjust quantities to meet MOQ
 
3. Flexibility & Exceptions
Should MOQ rules be configurable by product category, customer, or sales channel?  Do you need exceptions (e.g., promotional orders, samples, special customers)?
 
4. Operational Impact
How should MOQ interact with other rules like pricing tiers, discounts, or shipping constraints? At what stage should MOQ be validated?
  • During order entry
  • At approval stage
  • At fulfillment
5. Example Scenario
Please provide a concrete example of an order where MOQ should apply (e.g., “Any order must have at least 100 units total, regardless of SKU mix”).

entry.title: Re: How to use BAPE_SDSALESDOCITEM to update custom VBAP...
entry.content:
Hi @bigflatfoot ,
Are you using Public Cloud Edition?

How do you call your coding? Are you using events? 

In general you could also use a Custom Application Job and update Sales Orders from Reference Documents via release RAP BO Interface I_SalesOrderTP. You can find an example here. This job could select the changed reference documents every day / hour / minute via release CDS Views I_SalesContract.

If you want to have the update in real time, you could also use local event consumption and update Sales Orders immediately when the contract or quotation has been changed. You can still use the Application Job as a backup if the update from the local event handler fails (for example because the sales order is currently locked).

entry.title: How to: Mass Update of Material Group and Purchasing Group in the Material Master (Public Cloud)
entry.content:
This post describes a simple and repeatable approach to mass‑updating the Material Group and Purchasing Group in the material master using standard apps in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

 

The Mass Update Process consists of three main steps:

Step 1: Export Master Data – Products

Fiori App: Export Master Data – Products (F8324)

  • Select the required fields:
    • Material Group (MARA‑MATKL)
    • Purchasing Group (MARC‑EKGRP)
  • Include all material numbers that need to be changed
  • Export the data using “Export” to download the Excel file

Step 2: Maintain Data in Excel

  • Update the Material Group and Purchasing Group values in the Excel file
  • Side Note:
    • Material Group is defined on client‑level (plant‑independent)
    • Purchasing Group is define plant‑specific and must be maintained for each relevant plant

Step 3: Manage Mass Maintenance – Products

Fiori App: Manage Mass Maintenance – Products (F8326)

  • Start a new mass maintenance process
  • Upload the updated Excel file

 

  • SAP automatically determines the fields in scope
  • Start the process via “Start”

 

 

  • In status “Stopped for Review”, review the proposed changes
  • Confirm execution by selecting “Continue” twice

 

 

 

  • Once the process reaches “Completed”, all changes are applied and can be verified in the product master

 

Summary

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, mass changes to Material Group and Purchasing Group in the material master can be handled efficiently by using a three step approach. SAP supports the process with validation steps throughout the process chain. 

References

  • Export Master Data | SAP Help Portal
  • Manage Mass Maintenance | SAP Help Portal
  • Start Mass Maintenance | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: SAP Sapphire 2026: What the announcements really mean for ECC and S/4HANA customers
entry.content:
The five key building blocks

1. Autonomous Enterprise: Humans set the direction, AI executes.

The Autonomous Enterprise is the overarching concept behind everything SAP is announcing this year. The idea: decisions are based on real-time data, workflows run end-to-end, and AI continuously improves operational processes. Crucially for regulated industries, SAP emphasizes fully governed AI – meaning AI that is transparent and controlled. Especially when it comes to financial, operational, and regulatory decisions, this isn't just a marketing ploy, but a prerequisite for using these functions productively at all.

2. Joule Work: One interface for the entire company

Joule Work combines AI assistants and agents in a single workspace. Instead of switching between applications, users define what they want to achieve – the system then coordinates the steps, gathers information, and initiates workflows.

From a user's perspective, this is probably the most tangible change: less "where can I find that?" and more "what do I want to do?". Whether this actually reduces friction in everyday use depends on how well the agents are integrated into the respective business processes – and on which SAP edition the customer is running (more on this below).

3. SAP Autonomous Suite: The operational core

Across finance, supply chain, procurement, HR, and customer experience, assistants and agents collaborate to manage entire processes—not just individual tasks. The shift from "automating individual steps" to "end-to-end execution" is conceptually crucial.

In practice, this shifts routine work. Employees focus on decisions and exceptions instead of manually coordinating workflows.

4. Industry AI: Solutions that understand the industry context

Generic AI functions are often insufficient in regulated or highly specialized industries. With Industry AI , SAP combines applications, data, and AI building blocks to meet the specific requirements of individual industries.

For customers in pharmaceuticals, automotive, banking, utilities or the public sector, this is likely to be more relevant than broad cross-functional areas – provided that the respective industry solution is actually available and not just announced.

5. SAP Business AI Platform: The basis

The platform brings together business data, applications, and AI services in a single environment. It addresses a problem that most companies are familiar with from their own pilot projects: Getting a single AI use case up and running is feasible; operating it consistently, securely, and scalably is significantly more difficult. The platform is the prerequisite for transforming individual use cases into a viable operating model.

The honest question is: How much of this actually reaches ECC customers?

One must be realistic here. A large part of the announced AI functions – especially Joule Work, the end-to-end agent orchestration of the Autonomous Suite, and many Industry AI building blocks – are based on the modern SAP platform, usually S/4HANA Cloud (Public or Private Edition).

For ECC customers, this means specifically:

  • Mainstream maintenance for ECC 6.0 ends at the end of 2027; extended maintenance is available for an additional fee until the end of 2030.
  • The more ambitious AI promises cannot be implemented on ECC, or only to a very limited extent.
  • The Sapphire announcements increase the pressure to migrate to S/4HANA – but they do not change the known timeline.

This is no surprise, but rather the logical continuation of SAP's established strategy. Those still running on ECC should view these announcements less as "new features for me" and more as an additional reason to finalize their migration plans.

And what about S/4HANA customers?

Here too, a distinction is worthwhile, because not all S/4HANA is the same:

  • S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition will receive the new AI features the fastest and most completely.
  • S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition / RISE with SAP receives a large portion of the functions, depending on the release level and the chosen service model.
  • S/4HANA On-Premise traditionally has the least and slowest access to new AI features. Those who remain with this system should realistically assess which of Sapphire's promises will actually be relevant – and which will not.

For many customers, this reignites the debate about the right S/4 edition. The question of "cloud or on-premise?" increasingly determines the pace of innovation one can achieve.

Perhaps the most practical part: Agent-led Transformation

Perhaps the most important announcement from a customer perspective comes almost as an aside: SAP is using AI not only in operations, but also in the transformation itself. Joule Agents and Assistants are intended to automate tasks such as system analysis, data preparation, and test support.

This is particularly relevant for ECC customers facing an S/4 migration. Migrations rarely fail due to technical issues alone, but rather due to the effort required for analysis, cleansing, customization evaluation, and testing. If agent-led transformation measurably reduces these effort items, it's a practical lever – and not just a marketing ploy.

The important thing remains: Each customer should assess for their specific situation how much of this is already productively usable today and how much is merely a roadmap. A proof of concept on a real subsystem is more informative than any slideshow.

Conclusion

SAP Sapphire 2026 doesn't mark a break, but rather a consolidation of the strategy of recent years: AI becomes an operational component of the suite, not an optional add-on. The direction is clear, and the benefits for customers on modern editions are plausible.

For the majority of existing SAP customers – with ECC or older S/4 releases – the most important message is less technical than planning-related: The gap between "I'll stay on my current environment" and "I can use the new features" is widening. Agent-led transformation is intended to close this gap; whether it succeeds in practice will become clear in concrete projects over the coming months.

Anyone looking for a complete overview of all announcements can find it in the SAP Sapphire Innovation News Guide 2026 .

entry.title: Re: How to give logic for triggering a mail to quality ta...
entry.content:
Logic for Picking Mail IDs

1) Identify the Task Processor Type

  • If individual → pick directly from user master data (e.g., SAP HR infotype with email).
  • If department → resolve to a distribution list mapped to that department + plant.

2) Mapping Department to Distribution List
Create an Organizational Unit (OU) in PPOME (e.g., “Quality Team”). Assign departments under this OU. For each department, maintain a mapping table (custom Z-table or config) linking:

  • Plant
  • Department
  • Distribution List ID

3) Distribution List Setup
Use SO23 to create distribution lists (e.g., DL_QUALITY_1000). Add all relevant mail IDs for that plant’s department. Ensure naming convention is consistent (e.g., DL_<Dept>_<Plant>).

4) Logic in Notification Release

  • When task is released:
    • Check if processor = department.
    • Read plant from notification header.
    • Lookup mapping table for (Plant + Department).
    • Fetch distribution list ID.
    • Trigger mail to distribution list via SAPconnect/Outlook integration.

entry.title: Upgrading your SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor: Beyond TinyMCE version 4
entry.content:
Rich Text Editor in SAPUI5

The Rich Text Editor is an SAPUI5 control for data input and editing. It is used to enter rich text with different styles, colors, and formatting. The Rich Text Editor allows users to format text and add different types of elements, such as images and hyperlinks.

Screenshot of the SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor control

See details on

  • sap.ui.richtexteditor.RichTextEditor: Design, documentation, API reference and samples
  • RichTextEditor building block: Documentation and documentation in SAP Fiori Development Portal

Dependency on TinyMCE

For its implementation, this control uses the third-party component TinyMCE (see https://www.tiny.cloud/). When Tiny deprecates a version of TinyMCE, this version might still be in use in some versions of SAPUI5. Should issues arise, the SAPUI5 team tries to fix them by working around gaps. However, sometimes this is not possible, and updating TinyMCE is necessary, even in maintenance versions of SAPUI5. This is especially important for security, an area where SAPUI5 must address upcoming risks for all customers.

This dependency adds restrictions to the usage and support of the Rich Text Editor control, see the documentation. If your app uses a deprecated version of TinyMCE, the console shows a respective error message stating that this version of TinyMCE is deprecated and will be removed in future.

What happens in July 2026?

 

SAPUI5 will stop loading the deprecated TinyMCE version 4 by default.
Instead, it will load the latest available version.

This will happen in the following SAPUI5 versions:

  • SAPUI5 1.71   - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.71.62
  • SAPUI5 1.84   - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.84.42
  • SAPUI5 1.96   - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.96.28
  • SAPUI5 1.108 - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.108.0 

What does this mean for you?

As always, you benefit from improved features and qualities of the SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor. If, and only if, your app relies on TinyMCE version 4, the app will break until you use the EditorType property to explicitly stay with version 4. SAP does not recommend using deprecated software, as explained above.

What is planned for 2026?

The SAPUI5 team further plans to permanently remove TinyMCE version 4 from the SAPUI5 code later in 2026. This means there will be no way to load it anymore, no will workaround available. SAP strongly recommends removing any dependency to TinyMCE version 4.

Call to Action

Avoid any dependency on a specific (deprecated) version of TinyMCE to always benefit from the latest features and qualities in your SAPUI5 apps.

entry.title: ABAP AI - Chapter 3: We go agentic!
entry.content:
Summary

  • SAP Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities will be supported in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition in all releases as of 2021 with planned start in Q2/2026
  • SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities will be priced on a consumption basis via AI Units
  • ABAP Development Tools for VS Code are planned to be GA in Q2/2026
  • An ABAP MCP Server will be shipped in ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse and ABAP Development Tools for VS Code as of Q2/2026 enabling agentic ABAP development experience
  • An ABAP Custom Code Transformation agent focusing on the migration from SAP ECC towards SAP S/4HANA is planned for Q2/2026

Intro

Since the start of ABAP AI in early 2025 (“Joule speaks ABAP!”), the update in September 2025 (“ABAP AI – Chapter 2”), now it is time to deliver on the promises made at SAP TechEd 2025 (“Introducing the next era of ABAP development”) – time for chapter 3!

We are entering the agentic era of ABAP development: let's get started.

SAP Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities in lower releases

As of June 2026, Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities are offered through a running on SAP BTP. This hub service combines authentication, license check, access to SAP BTP AI Core and use case specific business logic and can be plugged directly to the developer’s IDE. This way, we can offer ABAP AI capabilities mostly independent of the ABAP backend release. This enables us to serve SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition on releases 2021 and higher with Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities.

  • Planned availability in Q2/26
  • Supported releases: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2021 and higher
  • Costs: See below
  • Configuration guide and further information will follow and will be linked here

New commercial approach for ABAP AI

The free promotion for J4D ABAP AI capabilities comes to an end in September 2026. Accordingly, it is now time to launch the commercial model: Based on AI units, ABAP AI capabilities are now priced purely based on consumption. This approach combines transparency regarding the real costs based on the usage and is well-suited for dynamic and individual agentic AI use cases.

  • Planned availability in Q2/26
  • Materials: 8019164 (customers) and 8019670 (partners)
  • Information on how to purchase and activate will follow soon and will be linked here

ABAP Development Tools for VS Code

14 years after the launch of ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse, time has come to support a new IDE. As of May 29th 2026, ABAP Development Tools will be available for VS Code. Building on the ABAP Language Server (see “Behind the Design…”), coupled with an ABAP MCP Server, this is the enabler for a truly agentic ABAP development experience, boosting development efficiency to the next level. It sets the foundation to leverage modern coding agents like GitHub Copilot directly for ABAP development. While we start with a small set of development objects and IDE tools focusing on SAP Fiori App development based on the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP), we will very soon enhance the scope of object types to serve high priority development scenarios.

The VS Code extension is

  • planned to be available as of Q2/26
  • Supported releases: release 7.31 and higher
  • Works in the Cloud and OnPrem
  • Costs: free of charge
  • Precise info on how to obtain and get started will follow soon and will be linked here

MCP Support for ABAP development

The Language Server provides the required abstraction to gain independence of the IDE sitting on top. A major component of this delivery is the MCP server for ABAP development. It provides powerful tools which help coding agents to perform complex ABAP coding tasks. While the Language Server is currently delivered as part of the ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse and for VS Code, a potential standalone-shipment of the Language and MCP Server is under discussion.

  • Release planned for Q2/26
  • Supported in Eclipse and VS Code
  • Works in the Cloud and OnPrem
  • Supported for release 7.31 and higher
  • Precise info on how to use it in the IDE will follow soon and will be linked here

Agentic custom code migration for the SAP S/4HANA transformation

Bringing all pieces together, we are launching the first ABAP custom code agent assisting in the transformation of custom code from ECC towards SAP S/4HANA. The required capabilities are offered through the ABAP Language and MCP Server explained above and can be used leveraging a coding agent, for example Amazon Q or GitHub Copilot. Currently, Eclipse must be used for this agent, until all required ABAP object types are supported in VS Code (see above). This agent boosts our customers on the RISE journey when migrating their classic landscape to the Cloud ERP.

  • Availability planned for Q2/26.
  • Supported in Eclipse
  • Prerequisites: Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities via the new commercial approach with AI units (see above) and coding agent on top (e.g. Amazon Q or Github Copilot)
  • Further information to follow soon and will be linked here

 

Finally - please have a look at our FAQ document for frequent questions and watch out for the GA releases of these deliverables. We will keep you posted.

Get yourself ready to dive into agentic ABAP development! 😎

entry.title: Virtual Elements Updated from action

entry.title: Re: Best Practices Recommendations for SAP Datasphere
entry.content: I fully agree with separating authorisations into a single space, and one suggestion SAP should take into account is that, in the intelligent apps it is proposing to customers, there is a Central Permissions table that is used in the ingestion space rather than being placed in a separate space. Why am I mentioning this? Because when you want to customise the harmonisation and analytics spaces, you have to make a copy of them, and in the copy, you may not want to have the same level of authorisations as you have in the standard intelligent app, so authorisation maintenance is duplicated. And if, on top of that, you want a separate space to build all your customisation and keep it separate from the copy, you have to set up another level of authorisations in that space, meaning maintenance is tripled. What would be the solution? In SAP Intelligent Applications, generate the DACS and the Central Permissions table in a separate authorisation space, so that these authorisations can be shared across the different usage spaces. Wouldn’t that be more optimal?

entry.title: Re: Controller Extension method not called in adaptation...
entry.content:
I had a similar problem while extending My Travel Requests V2 standard fiori app  - F0409A. I solved it by changing the controller I was extending in the codeExt file. Depending on where you click to create the controller extension, it might reference the Controller for the reuse component instead eg create extension for a form field instead of the details page. so in your codeExt file update the selector to the controller of the app you are currently in, in my case it was the following.

"selector": {
"controllerName": "fin.travel.mytravelrequestsv2.controller.Main"
},

remember to like if it helps...

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #1: Why Utilities Need an Intelligent Core on SAP BTP Introduction Utilities are
entry.content:
Introduction

Utilities are entering a decade defined by real‑time operations, distributed energy, and AI‑driven decisioning. Yet most utility architectures were never designed for this level of speed or complexity. The result is a widening gap between what the grid needs and what legacy systems can deliver.

Many challenges come not from SAP itself, but from the surrounding legacy integrations and custom logic that slow down data and processes

As the industry accelerates toward grid modernization, AMI 2.0, and DER integration, A common pattern is becoming clear: architectural constraints around the ERP often limit real‑time operations more than the ERP system itself:

The ERP is no longer the bottleneck—the architecture is.

Even with SAP S/4HANA, utilities may still encounter challenges such as:

  • Delayed response to real-time grid events
  • Data is trapped in silos between the field (OT) and the office (IT).
  • Rigid, high-cost integrations
  • The inability to scale AI beyond pilots

The missing element is an Intelligent Core—a layer that coordinates events, applies business logic, and supports AI‑driven operations.  An Intelligent Core is a real-time orchestration layer that sits between edge systems and ERP, managing events, applying business logic, and enabling AI-driven operations.

1. The Architecture Gap: Complexity Without Coordination

Today’s utility landscape is a tightly coupled mesh of embedded logic, batch processing, and siloed systems. Data moves slowly, intelligence is fragmented, and every change increase complexity.

At its core, this environment is defined by:

  • Embedded Logic: Custom ABAP tightly coupled within ERP
  • Batch Latency: Critical data processed in delayed cycles
  • Siloed Intelligence: AMI, MDM, and grid data isolated from business processes

The result is predictable:

  • Slow response to operational events
  • High cost of change
  • Limited ability to innovate

The challenge is less about individual systems and more about how they coordinate across the landscape.

2. Reimagining the Core: From Record to Orchestration

A modern core is not just a system of record—it is a system of orchestration.

The Intelligent Core acts as the enterprise’s central nervous system, sitting between the edge (meters, grid, customer channels) and the back office (S/4HANA). It performs functions the ERP was never designed to handle:

  • Real-Time Event Orchestration: Reacting to events as they occur
  • Data Normalization: Validating and standardizing inputs before processing
  • Business Rule Execution: Applying logic consistently across systems
  • AI Enablement: Embedding intelligence directly into operational flows

In this model:

  • S/4HANA remains the System of Record
  • SAP BTP becomes the System of Intelligence

3. Why SAP BTP Is the Foundation

The Intelligent Core should never live inside S/4HANA. It requires a platform designed for real-time processing, modular services, and distributed integration.

SAP BTP provides that foundation.

More importantly, it enforces a critical architectural principle:

S/4HANA handles transactions, while BTP handles orchestration, intelligence, and integration.

Figure 1: Intelligent Core Architecture for Utilities

 

This separation enables:

  • Clean Core by Design
  • Event-Driven Agility
  • Modular Services
  • Operational AI
  • Unified Integration Layer

 In this architecture, BTP plays a central role in enabling real‑time orchestration and modular services. In practice, the Intelligent Core leverages SAP Event Mesh for asynchronous event routing, SAP Integration Suite for secure connectivity, and CAP‑based microservices deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. Business rules can be modeled using SAP Business Rules Service, while AI Core orchestrates model execution and inference. Together, these components form a modular, event‑driven stack that scales independently of the ERP.

4.       Conceptual Flow: From Data to Action

The shift in architecture is simple—but powerful:

  • Ingest: Raw events from AMI, grid systems, and customer channels flow into Event Mesh
  • Process: The Intelligent Core standardizes data, applies business rules, and classifies events using AI
  • Execute: S/4HANA receives only clean, validated, business-ready inputs

This changes the game:

  • From raw data → business events
  • From batch → real-time
  • From system-centric → flow-centric

For example, a meter‑read anomaly event published from AMI enters Event Mesh, triggering a CAP service that validates payload structure and enriches it with master data from HANA Cloud. The service then invokes an AI Core endpoint to classify the anomaly type before routing the result to a workflow service that updates the billing process in S/4HANA. Each step is asynchronous, traceable, and governed through BTP’s event and API management layers.

Below diagram shows how AMI Meters and grid systems send event data into SAP BTP’s Intelligent Core for real‑time ingestion, enrichment, AI‑based classification, and workflow orchestration before validated business events reach SAP S/4HANA for transactional processing and analytics.

Figure 2: End‑to‑End Utility Event Flow — AMI → SAP BTP → SAP S/4HANA 

5.  Real-World Impact: Exception Handling Reimagined

The Old Model: Inconsistent AMI data flows into ERP → Billing exceptions increase → Manual teams intervene → Delays and errors compound

The Intelligent Core Model: Events enter the core → Data is normalized and enriched → AI classifies and resolves known patterns → Only true exceptions reach humans

The Result:

  • Higher billing accuracy
  • Reduced operational overhead
  • Faster meter-to-cash cycles

The Intelligent Core integrates seamlessly with SAP Workflow Management and Business Process Automation, allowing utilities to embed exception‑resolution logic directly into operational processes without modifying the ERP.

6.  The Bottom Line

Utilities do not modernize by upgrading ERP alone. They modernize by re-architecting how systems respond, coordinate, and learn.

The shift is clear:

  • From rigid, transaction-centric systems
  • To event-driven, intelligence-driven architecture

SAP BTP becomes a key platform for enabling event‑driven, intelligent operations.

7.  Technical Reference (for practitioners)

  • SAP Event Mesh — Real‑time event routing
  • SAP Integration Suite — Secure connectivity and API management
  • SAP CAP — Modular service development
  • SAP AI Core — Model orchestration and inference
  • SAP Workflow Management — Process automation and exception handling
  • SAP HANA Cloud — Unified data foundation

 

Next in the Series

ATUL Architecture #2 — Event‑Driven Utilities: Designing Real‑Time Flows on SAP BTP.  We’ll walk through:

  • AMI → BTP → S/4HANA event flow
  • Event Mesh patterns
  • Real‑time vs batch
  • A clean, simple event‑flow diagram

entry.title: Re: Practice System: Fresh Start Option for Repeated Lear...
entry.content:
Dear @rjones0923,

Based on SAP current Practice Systems information learners can repeat the exercises during the active practice-system access period but SAP does not document a learner-controlled reset option or an automatic clean-slate reset after each session. SAP describes practice systems as course-specific environments with exercises, guided tasks and pre-set scenarios. Access is available only within the assigned system time frame and SAP confirms that learners may continue practicing within that validity period, with no 60-hour usage limit

For data separation, SAP’s documented mechanism is the assigned user group number. In shared practice systems, learners are instructed to replace ## or XX in the exercises with their own assigned group number so that their data is separated from other users’ data

The only SAP-documented follow-on option is re-enrollment after the current enrollment’s end date. SAP states that the enrollment end date cannot be extended, but learners can enroll again afterwards. However, SAP does not explicitly confirm that re-enrollment automatically provides a fresh system instance or clean dataset

Therefore I would not position the Public Cloud practice system as supporting an on-demand reset or automatic clean slate for each new practice session. Learners can repeat the exercises but entries created in previous attempts may remain during the same active enrollment. There is also no documented reset action that learners can initiate themselves. The practical approach is to repeat exercises using the assigned group number and where needed, create new exercise-specific object names or manually reverse/delete test data if the exercise and authorizations allow it

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/helpcenter/learninghub-subscription/practice-systems

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Automatic Purchase order generate during MIGO posting
entry.content:
Business requirement:(SAP S4 Hana Public Cloud)

Multiple materials will be received daily from different vendors (suppliers) per production requirements. The user will not get sufficient time to create a purchase order every day and process it further. So they are directly accepting the material. 

So in the back end, the purchase order should be created automatically once the GRN is posted.

  • Pre-requisite:
    • Standard Purchasing Org assignment with Plant.
    • Automatic PO flag at movement type (Missing in Public Cloud)
    • The material master Auto PO flag should be checked.
    • Supplier master Auto PO flag should be checked.
    • Purchase Info record must be maintained.
    • Goods receipt
      • Transaction: Goods receipt
      • Reference: Other
      • Movement type: 101/501.
  • Result:
    • The purchase order should be created automatically.

entry.title: Re: MRP run considers substitute inventory
entry.content:
Hi Sravan,
Materials MAT_A, MAT_B and MAT_C are assigned to the same alternative item group. However, the current SAP MRP run does not consider the stock of alternative materials MAT_B and MAT_C, and only checks the inventory of the primary material MAT_A with priority 1.

This results in purchase requisitions still being generated for MAT_A even when usable stock is available for MAT_B and MAT_C, leading to inventory stagnation. Therefore, we need the system to take the stock of alternative materials into account during MRP calculation.

Thanks.
@sravan_aleshwaram

entry.title: Unlocking Potential: Why Continuous Learning is the Engine of Progress
entry.content:
I remember a conversation I had with a new manager several years ago that left a lasting impact on me. Instead of starting with immediate tasks or deliverables, she asked, "What is your ideal vision for the future? What new skills are you eager to explore? And how can I help you achieve that?" That discussion highlighted my observations: continuous learning is not just about fulfilling requirements - it is a key driver of personal and professional growth. More than merely acquiring facts and figures, it cultivates a fundamental mindset of open-mindedness and flexibility, enabling us to quickly sense the environment, embrace new ideas, and discern fresh opportunities. This capacity to adapt and integrate new information seamlessly is what truly sets individuals and organizations apart. That's why making the most of every moment is so important to me – tuning into podcasts and news during my drives to Walldorf or walks in the park, staying updated and focused on what truly matters.

Today’s unpredictable landscape, marked by heightened geopolitical tensions across regions like the Middle East, Eastern Europe, and the Indo-Pacific, persistent supply chain disruptions, and the stark lessons from recent global challenges like the pandemic, proves that stability is rare. In such a volatile environment, continuous learning is the ultimate determinant of survival and success. When emergencies erupt, the rapid ability to learn and adapt becomes the essential mechanism to navigate, innovate, and thrive. For industries like Oil, Gas, and Energy, this means cultivating the ability to sense, adapt, and see new possibilities—beyond just accumulating facts—to find solutions immediately and emerge stronger. This inherent flexibility brings a crucial sense of calm amidst chaos.

At SAP, we embody this philosophy, believing that to truly "teach what we preach," we must first live it. Our commitment to continuous learning extends both within the company and externally to our valued customers and partners in industries like Oil, Gas, and Energy. We recognize that staying ahead requires constant adaptation, fresh perspectives, and keen environmental awareness, which is why we’ve implemented a multitude of enablement initiatives throughout the year.

Internally, programs like Learn2Win, which took place last week, are designed to empower our industry colleagues around the globe with the latest and most important insights, ensuring our teams are always at the forefront of innovation.

Externally, we deeply value our customers' and partners' time. Beyond extensive virtual and on-site classroom training, we offer specialized sessions, such as our "Espresso Sessions for Energy and Utilities". These short, focused "coffee break" formats, typically 25-30 minutes long, are perfect for sharing the newest developments in Up, Mid, Downstream, and other relevant cross-industry areas. To further facilitate learning and dialogue, we also organize significant industry events such as the recent “SAP Energy and Utilities Conference” in Toulouse, which provided a powerful platform for learning not only from SAP experts but also from industry peers. Looking ahead, don’t miss our upcoming Innovation Days in Oklahoma City this August and Houston this October, offering deep dives into critical industry topics.

Additionally, to help you stay tuned to recent changes, technological updates, industry directions, announcements, new programs and channels, and upcoming and on-demand events, we publish the SAP Oil, Gas, and Energy newsletter bi-weekly, delivering curated insights straight to your inbox.

To stay even more connected with us, the industry, and the latest from SAP, we encourage you to explore our vibrant SAP Community group, follow the topic on LinkedIn, and delve deeper into how SAP supports your operations within the Oil, Gas, and Energy business on our Industry Solutions page.

Your journey of continuous learning is our shared mission, and we’re always eager to hear your ideas and the new topics you’d like us to explore together. Reach out and share your thoughts!

entry.title: Extending Business Process Monitoring with Custom Processes in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Introduction 

Business Process Monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM for Operations provides a comprehensive set of predefined processes and KPIs covering standard end-to-end scenarios across SAP solutions. 

However, many customers operate custom or company-specific processes that go beyond the standard content delivered. SAP Cloud ALM allows you to seamlessly integrate such custom content—including custom processes and custom KPIs—into the monitoring framework. 

To support these individual business needs, SAP Cloud ALM enables you to create, import, and monitor custom processes and to enrich them with both standard and custom KPIs. 

This blog post explains: 

  • How custom processes are created manually 
  • How custom processes can be imported from an existing process hierarchy 
  • How KPIs can be assigned to custom processes 

All steps are demonstrated from the perspective of Business Process Monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM Operations. 


Custom Process Structure 

Processes modeled during implementation (in the ALM suite) can be reused to reflect your company’s actual business processes. 
Custom processes can be structured flexibly using multiple hierarchy levels. Instead of fixed-layer terminology, SAP Cloud ALM uses a hierarchy of nodes and child nodes, allowing you to model structures as needed. 


Creating vs. Importing Custom Processes 

You can either: 

  • Create custom processes manually from scratch, or 
  • Import them from an existing Process Hierarchy 

Besides, creating a process manually, importing existing structures is often the next logical step—for example, to accelerate setup or reuse already modeled business content. 

Importing Custom Processes 

In addition to manual creation, you can import the process hierarchy from the Implementation area.
Processes modeled during the implementation phase can be reused to reflect your company’s actual business processes. 

This is especially useful for: 

  • Reusing best practices or already modeled templates 
  • Accelerating initial setup 
  • Ensuring alignment with your company’s existing ALM process landscape 

 

Navigation & Authorizations 

The import is available in: 
Business Process Monitoring → Configuration → Import Custom Process 

This action typically requires appropriate authorizations in SAP Cloud ALM. 

Review and Adjust Imported Processes 

After import, you should review the structure and refine the selection of imported nodes. 

It is important to note: 

  • During import, you define which parts of the hierarchy are included 
  • You can later refresh or re-import if the original hierarchy changes 
  • If descriptions or structures are modified manually, the process becomes decoupled from the original hierarchy 

This allows flexibility, while still supporting reuse of centrally maintained content. 

Assigning KPIs to Custom Processes 

Monitoring becomes valuable once KPIs are assigned to process nodes. 

Custom processes allow you to combine standard SAP KPIs with custom KPIs, enabling you to fully reflect your company-specific monitoring requirements. 

Create or Select a Custom KPI 

Custom KPIs extend monitoring beyond standard content and are designed to reflect customer-specific requirements.

Activation and Visibility 

Once the custom process and assigned KPIs are activated: 

  • They become visible in the Business Process Monitoring overview 
  • They behave like standard SAP-delivered content 
  • They enable monitoring of business scenarios not covered by standard content 

Custom KPIs are especially valuable when standard monitoring is not sufficient - in combination with custom processes, they provide a complete solution tailored to your business. 

 

After activation, custom processes are seamlessly integrated into Business Process Monitoring and become immediately visible alongside standard content.

Defined KPIs are collecting data, and corresponding events can be configured to trigger alerts based on your business thresholds.

 

After activation, you can verify KPI assignments across all nodes and ensure that monitoring coverage aligns with your business processes. KPIs start collecting data immediately, while events provide visibility into critical situations. This ensures a consistent, process-driven monitoring setup that reflects your real business operations and enables targeted responses.

In addition, the process structure is reflected in Access Groups, ensuring that responsibilities and visibility align with your organizational setup. This allows teams to monitor and respond to issues within the exact process context for which they are responsible, enabling efficient, targeted operations.

Your Turn 

Now it is your turn to: 

  • Identify relevant custom business processes 
  • Decide whether to create or import them 
  • Enhance them with meaningful KPIs (standard and custom) 

Start extending Business Process Monitoring based on your individual business requirements. 

Summary 

Custom processes in SAP Cloud ALM provide maximum flexibility for Business Process Monitoring. 

By combining custom process structures with standard and custom KPIs, you can fully align monitoring with your real business execution—beyond standard content. 

For further details, it is recommended to consult: 

  • SAP Help Portal 
  • SAP Cloud ALM Expert Portal 

Feel free to use the commenting area to share feedback or best practices from your own implementation.

entry.title: Re: Unable to Launch SAP Learning Hub Exercises – Trainer...
entry.content:
Dear @Apsara_Janagan1,

This looks like a SAP Learning Hub Practice Systems / trainer environment technical issue, not a problem with the Sourcing and Procurement course content itself. SAP confirms that practice systems are course-specific environments with exercises, guided tasks and pre-set scenarios. After enrollment, some systems may need preparation time before they are ready in some cases up to 90 minutes. However, once the system is running and ready the exercises should be accessible. If multiple exercise/trainer pages keep loading and the browser becomes unresponsive this should be handled as a technical access issue for the assigned practice system

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/helpcenter/learninghub-subscription/practice-systems

Recommended checks before raising the case:

  • Clear browser cache and cookies
  • Restart the browser after clearing the data
  • Try another supported browser for example Chrome or Edge
  • Try Incognito/InPrivate mode
  • Disable browser extensions such as ad blockers, script blockers or privacy blockers
  • Allow pop-ups and redirects for SAP Learning pages
  • Test from another network, especially if you are using a corporate VPN, proxy or restricted company network

If the issue still occurs for multiple exercises, I would raise a case through the SAP Learning Help Center and select Practice Systems as the service category. SAP Practice Systems support page confirms that support cases should include detailed information so the support team can investigate the issue

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/helpcenter

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2404504

In the case, include:

  • Learning Journey / course name
  • Practice system or exercise name
  • Exact URL, if visible
  • Date and time with time zone
  • Browser and browser version
  • Screenshot of This page isn’t responding
  • Confirmation that the issue happens for multiple exercises
  • Information whether VPN, proxy or corporate network is used

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: I can't enter Valuation Type in GR Other (Movement Ty...
entry.content:
Hi @ayya ,
My friend movement type 501 cannot accept valuation type (BWTAR) for split‑valuated materials — by SAP design. That’s why OMJJ won’t let you activate the field, and why the system throws MICK013 when you try to choose a valuation type

You can do following as mentioned below.

Use movement type 561 (Initial Stock) instead of 501. movement type 561 does allow valuation type for split‑valuated materials. Use this if you are loading stock manually or correcting stock. You also use a PO-based GR (101). If the business process allows it, then create a PO with valuation type (C1/C2/C3). Post GR with 101, system will accept valuation type normally

Please accept my solution if you resolve your problem with my suggestion.

entry.title: How make fields in VA02 editable after an error or warning message is displayed?
entry.content:
Goal: I am trying to implement an error message when a line item is created in VA02 without values from VGPOS/VGBEL, then the user accidentally saves the document-- an error or warning message has to be displayed then that specific line item has to be modifiable.

Replication steps:
1. In VA02, create a new line item with a POSNR value of 120,000

2. The user then tried to save the document immediately. THe warning message is displayed, but instead of line 120,000 being modifiable--it is currently in read-only mode UNLESS the user will double click it. Additionally, the top most line item was highlighted by SAP instead of the line 120,000.

So my concern, is it possible to redirect the standard's logic of SAP from highlighting the top most line item into my current line item with error. And that line item has to be readily modifiable without pressing enter or double click.

entry.title: n8n on SAP BTP Kyma — Part 1: From Local Docker to Enterprise-Grade Deployment
entry.content:
Technology Blogs | SAP Community | n8n meets SAP — Part 1 of 3

Enterprise Automation — Deploying n8n on SAP BTP Kyma

A Step-by-Step Guide to Hosting n8n in a Cloud-Native SAP Environment

Technology Blogs · n8n  ·  SAP BTP  ·  Kyma  ·  Kubernetes
 
Series Overview:
  1. Enterprise Automation: Deploying n8n on SAP BTP Kyma 📍
  2. Create a Custom SAP AI Core Chat Model Node in n8n
  3. Workflow n8n Integration with Joule Studio

1. The Big Picture

If you have ever run n8n on a local machine or a simple VM, you already know how powerful it is. A few clicks, a running container, and you have a workflow automation engine at your fingertips. But when it comes to real enterprise use,  shared access, uptime guarantees, integration with SAP systems, and data that cannot simply disappear when a server restarts, a more robust foundation is needed.

This is where SAP BTP, Kyma runtime comes in. Kyma is SAP's fully managed Kubernetes environment, running directly on the Business Technology Platform. Instead of you managing cluster infrastructure, SAP manages the control plane. You deploy workloads; SAP keeps the platform running. Your n8n instance gets all the benefits of Kubernetes — automatic restarts on failure, persistent storage, a stable external URL, and native proximity to other BTP services like SAP AI Core or SAP HANA Cloud — without the overhead of operating a cluster yourself.

This post comes with all the files you need. At the bottom of this post you will find the complete contents of the deployment files,  docker-compose.yml for local development, and n8n-pvc.yaml together with n8n-kyma.yaml for the Kyma deployment. You can create each file locally by simply copying the code provided. By the end of this post, you will have a live, publicly accessible n8n instance running inside your SAP BTP subaccount.

 

2. Provisioning the Infrastructure

Enabling Kyma Runtime in SAP BTP Cockpit

Before any deployment can happen, the Kyma runtime needs to be enabled in your SAP BTP subaccount. If you have not done this yet, the process is straightforward.

Log in to the SAP BTP Cockpit and navigate to your subaccount. In the left-hand navigation, open "Kyma Environment". If Kyma has not been provisioned yet, you will see an "Enable Kyma" button. Click it, choose a plan (for most scenarios the default plan is sufficient), and confirm. Provisioning takes several minutes — SAP is standing up a fully managed Kubernetes cluster on your behalf.

Once complete, the status in the Cockpit changes to "Created" and a "Link to dashboard" appears. That dashboard is your Kyma control plane — a Kubernetes-native UI where you can inspect namespaces, deployments, services, and more. Keep it open alongside your terminal throughout this guide.

 

 

SAP BTP Cockpit subaccount view showing the Kyma Environment tile with status "Created" and the "Link to dashboard" button visible.

 

 

 
 
 
 
 
The Kyma Dashboard landing page after clicking through — showing the cluster overview and the left-hand namespace selector.

 

 

A Note on Resource Sizing

n8n is a Node.js application. It is not particularly memory-hungry at rest, but it spins up processes to execute workflows, and those processes can be CPU-intensive depending on what your workflows do. For a shared team instance handling moderate workflow loads, a cluster with at least 2 vCPUs and 4 GB of memory available for your workloads is a comfortable starting point.

In Kyma's managed environment, the underlying node pool is managed by SAP. If you are on a trial account, be aware that trial clusters have resource limits. For any production or shared team usage, an enterprise subaccount with a properly sized instance type is the right choice. You can check available capacity in the Kyma Dashboard under the cluster's node details.

3. Establishing the Connection

Your cluster is running in the SAP cloud. Your deployment files are on your local machine. To bridge the two, you need two tools installed locally: kubectl (the Kubernetes command-line client) and a Docker engine (for building custom images — relevant in Part 2 of this series).

The Toolbelt

kubectl is the standard CLI for interacting with any Kubernetes cluster. Install it following the official guide for your operating system. On macOS, brew install kubectl is the fastest path. On Windows, the installer is available directly from the Kubernetes project.

Docker Desktop covers both the Docker engine and an optional local Kubernetes cluster for testing. Download it from docker.com. For this post, Docker is only needed for the local docker-compose option and for Part 2 — the Kyma deployment itself pulls images directly from Docker Hub.

The Key to the Kingdom: Downloading your Kubeconfig

kubectl does not know which cluster to talk to until you give it a configuration file — the kubeconfig. This file contains the cluster's API endpoint URL, authentication credentials, and context information. Kyma makes it easy to download.

In the BTP Cockpit, select Kyma Enviroment and click the "KubeconfigURL" . Save the file somewhere accessible, for example ~/.kube/kyma-config.yaml. Then tell kubectl to use it by setting the KUBECONFIG environment variable in your terminal:

export KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/kyma-config.yaml

For this to persist across terminal sessions, add the line to your shell profile (.zshrc, .bashrc, or equivalent).

Verification

With the kubeconfig set, run:

kubectl get nodes

If everything is configured correctly, you will see a list of the worker nodes in your Kyma cluster — their names, status (Ready), and age. This confirms that your local machine is communicating with the SAP cloud cluster. If you see an authentication error instead, double-check that the KUBECONFIG variable is set in the correct terminal session.

 

4. Deploying the Application

With the cluster accessible, it is time to deploy. The process has a deliberate order: storage first, secrets second, application third. Never the other way around — a Pod that starts without its volume or credentials will crash and require manual intervention.

Namespace Creation

Kubernetes clusters are shared environments. Namespaces are the mechanism for creating logical isolation within a cluster — separate resource quotas, separate access controls, separate network policies. Running n8n in its own n8n namespace keeps it cleanly separated from any other workloads you deploy later.

kubectl create namespace n8n
Always use a dedicated namespace. Deploying into default is a habit that causes confusion fast once you have more than one workload in a cluster.

Persistence First

n8n stores everything on disk: your workflow definitions, saved credentials, execution history, and configuration. Without a persistent volume, all of that disappears every time the Pod restarts — and in Kubernetes, Pods restart. For updates, node maintenance, or any crash, the container is replaced with a fresh one. Without persistence, you start from zero every time.

The n8n-pvc.yaml file in the repository defines a PersistentVolumeClaim — a request to the cluster for a dedicated 5 GB storage volume. Apply it first:

kubectl apply -f n8n-pvc.yaml

Kyma provisions the underlying storage automatically. You can verify it was created and is in Bound status with:

kubectl get pvc -n n8n
 

The Secret Sauce: The Encryption Key

n8n encrypts all credentials it stores — API keys, passwords, OAuth tokens — using an encryption key that you provide via the N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY environment variable. This key is critical. If you lose it, n8n can no longer decrypt any of the credentials you have saved, and you will need to re-enter them all from scratch.

Generate a secure key using:

openssl rand -hex 32

Copy the output. Now create a Kubernetes Secret to hold it safely — secrets in Kubernetes are stored separately from your application manifests and are not exposed in plain text in your deployment files:

kubectl create secret generic n8n-secret \
  --from-literal=N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY='<your-generated-key>' \
  -n n8n
Never put a real key directly into a YAML file that you commit to a repository. The .env.example file in the repo shows the expected format for local development — use that for Docker Compose, and use Kubernetes Secrets for Kyma.

Deploying n8n

With storage claimed and the secret created, apply the main deployment manifest:

kubectl apply -f n8n-kyma.yaml -n n8n

The n8n-kyma.yaml file defines a Kubernetes Deployment — a declaration of the desired state for the n8n application. It tells Kubernetes to run one Pod using the official n8nio/n8n:latest image, mount the persistent volume at /home/node/.n8n (where n8n writes all its data), and inject a set of environment variables including the host domain, protocol, port, and CORS origins.

Before applying, open n8n-kyma.yaml and replace the two placeholder values:

Placeholder What to put here
<your-encryption-key>The key you generated above — or better, reference the Kubernetes Secret
<your-kyma-domain>Your Kyma app domain, e.g. n8n.abc1234.kyma.ondemand.com

Once applied, Kubernetes pulls the n8n image from Docker Hub and starts the container. Track the startup with:

kubectl get pods -n n8n --watch

The Pod status will move from Pending (image being pulled) to ContainerCreating to Running. Once it shows Running and 1/1 in the Ready column, n8n is up.

 

5. Creating the Service — The Internal Bridge

At this point n8n is running inside a Pod. But Pods in Kubernetes are ephemeral — every time the Pod is restarted, recreated, or updated, it gets a new internal IP address. Any other component trying to reach n8n by IP would break on the next restart.

A Kubernetes Service solves this. It is a stable, named internal endpoint that always points to the correct Pod, regardless of how many times that Pod has been replaced. The Service does not change; the Pod behind it can.

Create a file called n8n-service.yaml and apply it to the cluster. The most important part of the Service definition is the selector:

selector:
  app: n8n

This tells the Service: "route traffic to any Pod that carries the label app: n8n." If you look at n8n-kyma.yaml, the Pod template carries exactly that label — that is how the Service and the Deployment find each other. External traffic arriving at the Service on port 80 is forwarded to the n8n container on port 5678.

kubectl apply -f n8n-service.yaml -n n8n
 
The Kyma Dashboard Services view in the n8n namespace, showing n8n-service listed with its cluster IP.

 

 

6. The API Rule — The External Gateway

The Service we just created is internal. It is reachable within the cluster, but not from your browser. The final piece is exposing n8n to the outside world through Kyma's API Gateway.

Kyma uses a custom resource called an APIRule to manage external access to services. An APIRule is more than just an ingress route — it integrates with Kyma's Istio service mesh and the Oathkeeper access control layer. This means you can add authentication policies directly at the gateway level, before a request ever reaches your application.

Creating the API Rule via the Kyma Dashboard

The simplest way to create an APIRule for a first deployment is directly through the Kyma Dashboard UI:

  1. Open the Kyma Dashboard and switch to the n8n namespace using the namespace selector at the top.
  2. In the left-hand navigation, find "API Rules" under the Networking section.
  3. Click "Create API Rule".
  4. Fill in the form:
    • Name: n8n
    • Service: select n8n-service, port 80
    • Host: choose a subdomain — n8n is a natural choice. Kyma will append your cluster's base domain automatically, resulting in a URL like https://n8n.abc1234.kyma.ondemand.com.
    • Access Strategy: set to NoAuth for the initial setup.
 

A Word on the Access Strategy

Setting the access strategy to NoAuth means Kyma's gateway passes all requests through to n8n without performing any additional authentication check at the gateway level. This does not mean the instance is unsecured — n8n has its own built-in user management and requires account login before anyone can see or edit workflows.

For a first deployment and internal team use, this is a perfectly reasonable starting point. For production environments or public-facing instances, you can tighten this by configuring the APIRule to use JWT validation against SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS), restricting access to authenticated SAP BTP users before requests even reach the n8n container.

Verify External Access

Once the APIRule is created, copy the host URL from the dashboard and open it in your browser. You should be greeted by the n8n login screen. If the browser shows a connection error, give it 30–60 seconds — APIRules take a moment to propagate through the Istio gateway. A green checkmark on the APIRule in the Kyma Dashboard confirms it was processed successfully.

 

Browser showing the n8n login screen at the live Kyma domain URL — the confirmation that the full stack is working end-to-end.

 

 

 

 

docker-compose.yml : 
services:
  n8n:
    image: n8nio/n8n:latest
    restart: always
    ports:
      - "5678:5678"
    environment:
      - N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY=${N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY}
      - N8N_HOST=localhost
      - N8N_PROTOCOL=http
      - NODE_ENV=production
    volumes:
      - ./n8n_data:/home/node/.n8n

 

 

.env.example :
# n8n Encryption Key — generate with: openssl rand -hex 32
N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY=your-random-32-byte-hex-key-here

 

n8n-pvc.yaml : 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: n8n
  namespace: n8n
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: n8n
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: n8n
    spec:
      securityContext:
        runAsUser: 1000
        fsGroup: 1000
      containers:
      - name: n8n
        image: n8nio/n8n:latest
        env:
          - name: N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY
            value: "<your-encryption-key>"

          - name: N8N_HOST
            value: "<your-kyma-domain>"
          - name: WEBHOOK_URL
            value: "https://<your-kyma-domain>/"
          - name: N8N_PROTOCOL
            value: "https"
          - name: N8N_PORT
            value: "5678"

          - name: N8N_CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS
            value: "https://<your-kyma-domain>/"

          - name: N8N_USER_FOLDER
            value: "/home/node/.n8n"
        volumeMounts:
          - name: n8n-data
            mountPath: /home/node/.n8n
      volumes:
      - name: n8n-data
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: n8n-pvc

 

n8n-kyma.yaml: 
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: n8n-pvc
  namespace: n8n
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi

 

 

AI Usage Disclosure: Gen AI was used exclusively for linguistic refinements such as grammar, spelling, and phrasing as well as for the structural organisation of this text. All conceptual content, technical knowledge, architecture decisions, and implementation steps were developed independently by the author.

 

 

Up Next — Part 2 of 3

Create a Custom Language Model Node for SAP AI Core

You now have a live, persistent n8n instance running on SAP BTP. In Part 2, we take it further: building a custom n8n node that connects directly to SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub — turning your automation platform into an AI-capable engine that can call SAP-hosted language models like GPT-4o or Llama 3 from within any workflow. The full source code is already available; we will walk through exactly how it works and how to bundle it into your Kyma deployment.

entry.title: Re-thinking Product Lifecycle Profitability with SAP IPD and Vianta PLP
entry.content:
Welcome to this blog post! In today's edition, I want to outline how companies can rethink product life cycle profitability using SAP PLM solutions like Cloud ERP or SAP Integrated Product Development (SAP IPD) integrated with Vianta PLP, a new cloud-native solution purpose-built to move beyond spreadsheets and fragmented cost data. 

No time to read? Register to access the recording of our latest Meet the Expert webinar via the SAP Supply Chain Management Enablement Hub here.

Also, don't miss your Co-Innovation Opportunity to shape the future of PLP (details at the end of this blog)!

Product life cycle profitability is not a finance exercise that happens after the design is done. It is a strategic lever that must be active from the earliest stage of product ideation, before a single part is ordered and while the cost curve can still be shaped. Yet most companies today are running this process on outdated tools: spreadsheets, poorly organized data, and disconnected systems that cannot keep pace with shorter definition cycle times, shifting geopolitical demands, and the explosion of product complexity.

In this blog, you will find out how product & process-centric industries can:

  • Calculate highly complex product cost structures in real time, across bills of material with tens of thousands of items
  • Upload specifications and formulations as a basis for complex cost calculations
  • Manage structured, cross-functional collaboration within a single solution aligned to SAP workflows
  • Build and compare business cases across multiple scenarios with transparent KPIs
  • Adopt a 100% clean-core, BTP-native SaaS solution that fits seamlessly into existing SAP landscapes
  • Leverage AI assistance for predictive costing, cost optimization proposals, and natural language interaction (roadmap feature)

The Challenge: Profitability Needs to be Designed In, Not Discovered Later

Most product teams know their margins are under pressure. The challenge is acting on that knowledge early enough to matter. Companies face a dual mandate: compress product definition cycle times while maintaining profitability and compliance across increasingly complex, cross-functional processes. The fastest path forward is to bring cost structures, collaboration, and product data into one place and layer AI on top to remove friction.

The symptoms of the current state are familiar: cost engineers working in Excel files that cannot handle the computational complexity of real products, product controllers receiving cost data too late to influence design decisions, and procurement and supply chain teams operating in silos with no structured way to feed market pricing into early-stage calculations.

The consequences are measurable: missed business case targets, delayed RFQ responses, products that reach production without a clear profitability picture, and reactive cost-cutting after the design window has already closed. What is needed is a connected product costing layer that makes every cost assumption visible, traceable, and calculable in real time, from the first ideation sketch to the final business case decision.

The Solution: Capabilities That Make the Difference

High-Performance Calculation Engine Built for Real Complexity

The foundation of Vianta PLP is a calculation engine designed for the scale that real industrial products demand. Products with tens of thousands of items in their bill of material, where the cost calculation for a single leaf node may require 100 steps or more, return results in under one second. A full product portfolio can be calculated within minutes.

This is the gap that spreadsheets cannot close. The engine supports comprehensive costing sheets with percentage-based and quantity-based overhead definitions, time-specific parameters for different validity periods, and a built-in formula editor that allows teams to define highly tailored calculation logic, including conditional rules based on material groups, cost components, and custom fields.

[An image showing the costing sheet UI with a formula.]

Cost structures can be imported directly from SAP S/4HANA or SAP IPD, giving teams a realistic starting point based on similar products already in the system rather than building from scratch. Every change to the cost structure triggers an immediate recalculation, so the impact of design decisions, e.g., swapping a carbon frame for an aluminum one, upgrading a battery specification) is visible in real time, not days later.

[An image showing the data import from SAP S/4 HANA Public Cloud.]

Calculation versions allow teams to iterate systematically: compare a baseline import against an optimized variant, apply updated costing sheets, and track the delta between target cost and actual cost at every stage. A cost component split view organizes the full product cost into semantic groupings like material, production, freight, and overhead, giving decision-makers a clear picture of where cost is concentrated and where optimization potential exists.

[An image showing a cost structure after import.]

Structured Collaboration Across Functions and Company Boundaries

Cost design is a cross-functional process. Engineers, controllers, procurement specialists, supply chain experts, and customer-facing teams all carry information that affects product profitability. This information needs to flow in a structured, traceable way, not through email threads and shared drives.

Vianta PLP provides a collaboration layer built directly on SAP workflows. Teams can be defined per project, work items routed to the right stakeholders, and approval steps configured to match the company's governance requirements. RFQ documents, cost structures, and calculation versions are attached to the project and visible to all relevant parties from a single interface.

[An image showing a collaboration UI.]

The collaboration model extends beyond internal teams. Supplier collaboration, customer-facing pricing workflows, and intercompany cost reviews can all be structured within the same framework, with configurable workflow steps and stakeholder definitions. Because the solution runs on BTP and integrates natively with Cloud  ERP and SAP IPD, teams already working in these SAP solutions experience this as an extension of their existing environment. 

Business Case Decisions Grounded in Real Data

The output of the costing process is a business case decision: is this product worth building, at what price, for what volume, and with what expected return? Vianta PLP closes the loop between cost calculation and business case by providing a structured comparison of scenarios (e.g., base case, worst case, optimistic case) with year-by-year cost and revenue projections, net return, payback period, overhead ratio, and expected sales.

[An image showing the worst case/best case comparison.]

This moves the business case from a one-time presentation exercise built in a spreadsheet to a living model that can be updated as assumptions change, compared against actuals once production begins, and reused as the starting point for the next product generation. Because the business case is grounded in the same cost structure as the engineering calculation, there is no reconciliation gap between what the product manager modeled and what the cost engineer calculated.

After the business case decision, the model continues to deliver value: actual cost data from production can be compared against the designed cost structure, changes can be captured and versioned, and the next iteration of the product starts from a richer, more accurate baseline.

The Architectural Basis: 100% Clean Core, Built for Seamless Adoption

Vianta PLP is a cloud SaaS solution built entirely on SAP BTP, using SAP technologies and frameworks for data storage, integration, and UI. It is designed in full alignment with SAP's clean core architecture principles, without unnecessary technical debt or proprietary extensions that complicate upgrades.

[An image showing the system architecture for a joint use of Vianta PLP and SAP IPD.]

For existing SAP customers, the solution is designed to feel like switching on an additional capability within their current environment. For customers running SAP S/4HANA without SAP IPD, out-of-the-box connectors provide direct integration with SAP S/4HANA public cloud, private cloud, and on-premise deployments. Open APIs are provided for integration with non-SAP systems.

The user experience is built on SAP Fiori with additional third-party components for a modern, data-dense interface tailored to the specific needs of each persona with the AI assistant accessible throughout.

The planned timeline for early access is Q3 2026, with productive use targeted for Q1 2027.

And it Won’t end There - Here's a Glimpse of the Future

AI Assistance for Faster, Better Product Decisions

In the future, AI will be embedded throughout Vianta PLP not as a feature layer but as a core part of the solution. The planned PLP Assistant will be able to read an incoming RFQ document, extract the relevant data (e.g., customer, target price, volume assumptions, product specifications) and automatically create a corresponding project, including an initial cost structure populated by similarity search against prior calculations. (The images below are part of a visionary demo of the agent. The final product will be shaped based on customer feedback.)

[An image showing the PLP AI Assistant accessed directly from the UI.]

When a specification requirement cannot be met by existing price data, the assistant identifies the gap and sources pricing from external market data, bringing it into the calculation without manual intervention. Teams can ask the assistant to explain the current calculation in plain language: what are the total costs, how is the cost split between material and labor, what are the key cost drivers, and how does this compare to reference products?

[An image showing a dialog with the PLP AI Assistant checking cost structures against RIQ.]

When the calculation is above target, the assistant proposes concrete optimization options (e.g., alternative materials, component substitutions, design trade-offs), ranks them by impact and filters them by the constraints defined in the RFQ. Teams accept or reject proposals and see the updated cost reflected immediately.

[An image showing a dialog with the PLP AI Assistant giving recommendations.]

This is the difference between a system that stores data and a system that accelerates decisions. The goal is not to replace the cost engineer or the product manager but to compress the time between a new requirement arriving and a credible, optimized cost response going back.

In sum, what can you expect from this product in the future?

Cost Optimization Agent

  • An AI agent that proactively identifies cost reduction opportunities across the product portfolio and proposes specific actions with projected impact 

Quotation Calculation for Engineer-to-Order

  • Structured costing workflows for ETO and make-to-order scenarios, supporting the full quotation process

 FX and Tariff Simulation

  • Model the impact of currency fluctuations, additional tariffs, or supply chain disruptions on existing product cost structures

Product Variant Comparison

  • Calculate and compare multiple design alternatives side by side before a full BOM exists, providing cost feedback at the earliest point in the design process

Portfolio-Level Business Cases

  • Extend the business case model to cover entire product portfolios, with advanced KPIs and scenario comparison across product families

Conversational UX and Agentic AI

  • Full natural language interaction for process navigation, cost queries, and optimization, moving from AI assistance to AI-driven costing workflows

Conclusion

 Product life cycle profitability requires the right foundation: a calculation engine that handles real complexity, AI that accelerates decisions rather than adding noise, collaboration that keeps cross-functional teams aligned, and business cases grounded in actual cost data rather than spreadsheet assumptions. Vianta PLP, integrated with SAP Cloud ERP or SAP IPD and built on SAP BTP, brings these capabilities together in a clean-core SaaS solution designed for the pace and complexity that modern product organizations require.

Get Involved: Co-Innovation Opportunity

Vianta and SAP are actively looking for co-innovation customers to shape the product roadmap. This is an opportunity to influence scope, define AI use cases, validate the collaboration and business case capabilities against your real requirements, and gain early access ahead of general availability.

Your opportunity to shape the solution according to your needs

  • Influence the scope
  • Together with our experts, identify additional AI cases
  • Early access to the solution
  • Register for exchange & further updates via info@vianta.io

More Information

- SAP Supply Chain Management Enablement Hub

- SAP Integrated Product Development on SAP Community

- SAP IPD Roadmap Explorer

- Download the Vianta PLP info package and demos at www.vianta.io.

 Don't hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts.

- Follow me on LinkedIn and stay up to date with our latest enablement assets: https://www.linkedin.com/in/annette-garcia-lorenzo
